summary: Ronacher argues that open-source licensing has become fragmented and politically fraught, with GPLv3/AGPLv3 changes splintering ecosystems and making compatibility harder. He traces how GPLv3’s added restrictions (like anti-tivoization) pushed major platforms and companies away, creating practical lockouts in Apple products and app stores. He highlights how newer projects and communities increasingly avoid GPL altogether, favoring permissive licenses or using AGPL as a dual-licensing lever. GitHub’s license chooser, he says, worsens the situation by encouraging shallow, uninformed choices and even promoting incompatible options. The conclusion is a call for clearer, community-vetted licensing education and tooling, rather than simplistic selectors that deepen confusion.
key_points: GPLv3 introduced incompatibilities and political constraints that split the GNU/Linux ecosystem and hinder code sharing.
Anti-tivoization and app-store conflicts make GPLv3 impractical for modern device and distribution ecosystems.
AGPLv3 enabled dual-licensing strategies, shifting licensing from ideology to commercial leverage.
An anti-GPL movement grew from enforcement fatigue, FSF mistrust, and companies preferring permissive licenses.
GitHub’s license selector promotes low-effort choices and obscures complex compatibility tradeoffs.
money_quotes: GitHub is one of the pillars of Open Source development these days and yet it's also a place where there is more improperly than properly licensed software.
The fun effect of this now is that it's absolutely clear that there are two different worlds in which GNU and Linux are living.
The GPLv3 switch of projects primarily shows up because these pieces of software stop being updated in Apple products or get removed entirely.
But why is Apple so opposed to newer versions of the GPL?
The AGPLv3 was a terrible success, especially among the startup community that found the perfect base license to make dual licensing with a commercial license feasible.

From a purely pragmatic perspective: I spent over a year doing busybox license enforcement, and a dozen lawsuits later I'm still unaware of a SINGLE LINE OF CODE added to the busybox repository as a result...
Right now I believe, the license choosing wizard of GitHub is a shitty “solution” to the problem of people not adding licenses to their code.
key_insight: Modern open-source licensing has become a fragmented, politically charged compatibility maze, and simplistic tooling like GitHub’s license chooser makes that confusion worse rather than better.
